God calls Moses and Aaron not to convince Pharaoh, but to witness to him. Notice the strange setup: the LORD tells Moses upfront that Pharaoh will not listen, that His heart will be hardened, and that judgment will come anyway. This is not a failure of negotiation waiting to happen. This is a confrontation orchestrated by God Himself, where the outcome is known from the beginning. Moses is told he will be "like God to Pharaoh"—not claiming authority over Pharaoh, but speaking with God's authority into his presence. There is something almost unsettling about this commission. Moses is eighty years old. He could have stayed in Midian. Instead, he walks into the house of the most powerful man in the world to demand something the LORD has already determined Pharaoh will refuse. The obedience here is radical because it has nothing to do with the probability of success.

The first sign—Aaron's staff becoming a serpent—should have broken Pharaoh's resistance immediately. A wooden staff does not transform into a living creature through ordinary means. But Pharaoh's magicians replicate it through their own arts, and the small detail that Aaron's serpent swallows theirs barely registers. Pharaoh's heart hardens anyway. He does not refuse because the sign was unconvincing; he refuses because his will is set against God's. When Aaron turns the Nile to blood, the magicians do the same. The land becomes uninhabitable—the fish die, the stench is unbearable, the people cannot drink—and still Pharaoh "did not take any of this to heart." He simply retreats to his palace. What breaks a heart that will not be moved? What sign penetrates when each marvel only seems to deepen the resistance?

The text forces us to reckon with a harsh reality: God's signs and wonders do not automatically convert the human will. They can be witnessed, replicated, even explained away. The hardening of Pharaoh's heart is presented as God's doing, yet Pharaoh's refusal is also his own choice. Both are true in ways that strain our logic. What matters for us is this: we are called to obedience regardless of outcome, to faithful witness regardless of reception, and to trust that God's purposes move forward even—especially—when human opposition appears immovable. The plagues will continue. Seven days pass. God is not finished, and neither is the judgment that will reshape all of Egypt and secure the freedom of His people.
